whyExists: Before you can write a single line of Python, your computer needs to know what Python is. Right now, if you type 'python' into your terminal, your computer has no idea what you mean — it's like asking someone to read a book written in a language they've never seen. Installing Python gives your computer the Python interpreter: a program that can read your Python code, understand it, and execute it. Without this step, nothing works.
curiosityQuestion: Why do some computers already have Python, while others need it installed separately?
problemItSolves: Python installation puts three essential things on your machine: the interpreter (which runs your .py files), the standard library (thousands of built-in tools), and pip (the package installer for third-party libraries). Each of these is needed before you can do anything in Python.
realWorldAnalogy: Think of Python like a foreign language translator. Your computer only speaks machine code by default. Installing Python is like hiring a translator who fluently converts your Python instructions into commands your computer can actually execute. Without the translator, your instructions are meaningless.
simpleExplanation:
Here is what happens step by step:

1. You download the Python installer from python.org
2. The installer places the Python interpreter (a program called 'python3') into a folder on your system
3. It also puts pip (Python's package manager) on your system
4. It optionally adds Python to your PATH — which means your terminal can find python3 no matter which folder you're in

After installation, when you type 'python3 hello.py' in your terminal, the operating system finds the python3 interpreter, which reads your hello.py file and runs it.
syntaxExplanation:
# After installation, verify it works:
# In your terminal (command prompt / bash / zsh):

python3 --version
# Expected output: Python 3.x.x (e.g. Python 3.12.0)

pip3 --version
# Expected output: pip 23.x.x from /usr/lib/python3.x/...

# Run a quick test:
python3 -c "print('Python is working!')"
# Expected output: Python is working!

examples.title: Verifying Your Python Installation
examples.code:
# Step 1: Open your terminal (Mac/Linux) or Command Prompt (Windows)
# Step 2: Type the following and press Enter:

python3 --version

# You should see something like:
# Python 3.12.0

# If you see 'command not found', Python is not installed or not on your PATH
# On Windows, you may need to use 'python' instead of 'python3'
examples.explanation: The '--version' flag tells Python to print its version number and exit. If Python is installed and on your PATH, you'll see the version. If not, your terminal doesn't know where to find it.

examples.title: Your First Python Program via Terminal
examples.code:
# The -c flag lets you run a Python statement directly in the terminal
# without creating a file

python3 -c "print('Hello, World!')"

# Output:
# Hello, World!

# This proves Python can read your code and execute it immediately
examples.explanation: The '-c' flag (short for 'command') lets you pass a Python expression directly to the interpreter. This is a quick way to test that Python is working before you write your first .py file.

examples.title: Checking pip is Available
examples.code:
# pip is Python's package installer
# It comes bundled with Python 3.4+

pip3 --version

# Example output:
# pip 23.3.1 from /Library/Python/3.12/lib/python/site-packages/pip (python 3.12)

# pip lets you install any of the 400,000+ packages from PyPI:
pip3 install requests  # installs the 'requests' HTTP library
examples.explanation: pip (Pip Installs Packages) is the tool you use to add third-party libraries to Python. When you see tutorials saying 'pip install flask' or 'pip install numpy', this is the tool they mean. It's included with Python automatically.

visualDiagram:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Download Python from python.org] --> B[Run the Installer]
    B --> C{Add Python to PATH?}
    C -->|Yes| D[python3 command works globally in terminal]
    C -->|No| E[Must navigate to Python folder to run it]
    D --> F[Verify: python3 --version]
    E --> G[Re-run installer and check 'Add to PATH']
    F --> H{See version number?}
    H -->|Yes| I[✅ Installation Complete]
    H -->|No| J[Check PATH or reinstall]
```

stepByStepExecution.explanation: Always download Python from the official python.org website. Avoid third-party download sites. The site automatically detects your operating system and recommends the latest stable version.

stepByStepExecution.action: Download the installer for your OS
stepByStepExecution.explanation: Windows: download the .exe installer. macOS: download the .pkg installer. Linux: Python usually comes pre-installed; if not, use your package manager (sudo apt install python3 on Ubuntu).

stepByStepExecution.action: Run the installer — check 'Add Python to PATH'
stepByStepExecution.explanation: On Windows, the installer has a critical checkbox at the bottom: 'Add Python to PATH'. If you skip this, your terminal won't find python3 later. Check this box before clicking Install Now.

stepByStepExecution.action: Open a new terminal and verify
stepByStepExecution.explanation: Open a NEW terminal window (the old one won't see the PATH change). Type: python3 --version. You should see the version number. On Windows, try 'python --version' if python3 doesn't work.
Type: pip3 --version. You should see pip's version. This confirms you can install packages later. If pip isn't found, you may need to run: python3 -m ensurepip --upgrade

memoryDiagram.heap:
[Disk]
  /usr/local/bin/python3    ← interpreter executable
  /usr/local/lib/python3.12  ← standard library
  /usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages  ← installed packages

commonMistakes: Forgetting to check 'Add Python to PATH' on Windows — fix by re-running the installer and enabling this option
Installing Python 2 instead of Python 3 — Python 2 is end-of-life (2020). Always install Python 3.x
Closing and not reopening the terminal — PATH changes only take effect in new terminal windows. If python3 --version fails, open a new terminal first
Having multiple Python versions and using the wrong one — use 'python3.12' instead of 'python3' to be explicit, or use pyenv for version management
Installing packages globally when you should use a virtual environment — for projects, always create a venv (covered in the Modules & Packages lesson)
